summary: SKGLElement (WPF) leaks memory because its Dispose method does not call the base GLWpfControl.Dispose(), and the bundled OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.2.3 has a known memory leak fixed in 4.3.3.

bugSignals.errorMessage: Memory grows continuously when disposing and recreating SKGLElement instances; never reclaimed

stepsToReproduce: Observe memory growing without ever being reclaimed

repoLinks.description: Minimal WPF repro project demonstrating the memory leak

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The SKGLElement.Dispose method still does not call base.Dispose() in current main. OpenTK dependency is still at 4.2.3.

analysis.summary: SKGLElement.Dispose(bool) releases SkiaSharp-owned resources (GRContext, SKSurface, etc.) but never calls base.Dispose() on the GLWpfControl. Additionally, the pinned OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.2.3 has a known leak (opentk/GLWpfControl#126) fixed in 4.3.3. Both issues contribute to unbounded memory growth when SKGLElement instances are disposed and recreated.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because the reporter demonstrates clear memory growth with a repro project. The root cause is confirmed in source: Dispose(bool) at line 192 only calls Release() but never base.Dispose(). The upstream OpenTK fix in 4.3.3 compounds the issue. This is area/SkiaSharp.Views because SKGLElement is a WPF view control. Platform is os/Windows-Classic (WPF). A community PR (#3311) exists and is still open.

keySignals.text: when the SKGLElement is disposed it does not call the Dispose method of the base class
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter correctly identifies the missing base.Dispose() call as the root cause.

keySignals.text: older versions of OpenTK have this memory leak: opentk/GLWpfControl#126
keySignals.interpretation: Upstream dependency bug compounds the leak — GLWpfControl 4.2.3 has the leak, 4.3.3 fixes it.

keySignals.text: You can see the memory leak is gone with #3311
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter has already submitted a working fix as PR #3311 (updating OpenTK to 4.9.4 / GLWpfControl 4.3.3).

codeInvestigation.finding: Dispose(bool) calls Release() to clean up SkiaSharp resources (canvas, surface, renderTarget, grContext) but never calls base.Dispose(). The GLWpfControl base class holds native OpenGL resources that are leaked.
Release() nulls out and disposes SkiaSharp objects only. No base class cleanup is invoked here either.
OpenTK 4.3.0 and OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.2.3 are pinned for .NET Core+. The upstream fix for the memory leak (opentk/GLWpfControl#126) requires GLWpfControl >= 4.3.3.

workarounds: Use PR #3311's approach: override OpenTK dependency versions in your app project to OpenTK 4.9.4 and OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.3.3 via PackageReference with version override
nextQuestions: Does updating OpenTK introduce any breaking API changes that affect SKGLElement?
Should Dispose(bool) also call base.Dispose(disposing) even after the OpenTK update to follow proper disposal patterns?

resolution.hypothesis: Two compounding issues: (1) SKGLElement.Dispose does not call base.Dispose(), leaking GLWpfControl native resources, and (2) the bundled OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.2.3 has a known leak fixed in 4.3.3.

proposals.title: Update OpenTK dependencies per PR #3311
proposals.description: Bump OpenTK to 4.9.4 and OpenTK.GLWpfControl to 4.3.3 (or newer) in the WPF csproj. PR #3311 already implements this. The upstream fix in GLWpfControl 4.3.3 resolves the native resource leak and exposes Dispose properly.

proposals.title: Add base.Dispose() call in SKGLElement.Dispose(bool)
proposals.description: In addition to updating OpenTK, add a base.Dispose() call in the Dispose(bool) method to ensure proper disposal chain regardless of upstream version.

proposals.title: Workaround: override OpenTK version in app project
proposals.description: Users can add explicit PackageReference overrides for OpenTK 4.9.4 and OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.3.3 in their app csproj to get the upstream fix without waiting for a SkiaSharp release.

resolution.recommendedReason: PR #3311 already implements the fix. The upstream GLWpfControl 4.3.3 resolves the root leak. Adding base.Dispose() should be done as a companion change for correctness.

actionability.reason: Bug is well-documented with repro, root cause identified in source, and a community PR exists. Needs maintainer review of the OpenTK version bump for potential side effects.

actions.description: Apply bug, views, and Windows labels
Acknowledge the bug, point to workaround, note PR #3311 is under review
actions.comment:
Thanks for the thorough analysis and repro project — confirmed that `SKGLElement.Dispose` doesn't call `base.Dispose()` and the bundled OpenTK.GLWpfControl 4.2.3 has a known leak (opentk/GLWpfControl#126).

As a workaround, you can override the OpenTK versions in your app project:

```xml
<PackageReference Include="OpenTK" Version="4.9.4" NoWarn="NU1701" />
<PackageReference Include="OpenTK.GLWpfControl" Version="4.3.3" />
```

PR #3311 addresses this. We'll review it for potential side effects from the OpenTK version bump.

actions.description: Cross-reference SKGLElement threading issue #2872 which also involves GLWpfControl
